reasoning: An emergence score of 8.0 on the CDC IRAT has never been recorded for any influenza virus, with the historical maximum being 7.5. Reaching this threshold would effectively require the onset of a new pandemic or highly efficient human-to-human transmission, the baseline annual probability of which is typically 1-3%. Furthermore, the bureaucratic lag between data collection, expert convening, and the publication of an updated IRAT score is typically 1.5 to 2 months. This reduces the actual window for the necessary dramatic viral mutations to occur prior to January 1, 2026. Given the current H5N1 emergence score is low to moderate (5.79) with primarily mild spillover cases in humans and no sustained human transmission, the probability of an 8.0+ score by the end of 2025 remains very low. I align my estimate with the community consensus at ~2.2%.
